title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
tldr: Fast vector search that trades small accuracy loss for speed by pruning the search space instead of checking every vector.
plain_english: Exact vector search checks every stored vector against your query—fine for thousands, unusable for millions. Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithms build indexes that let you skip most vectors, returning nearly-as-good results in a fraction of the time. You get 95–99% of the top matches while running 10–100× faster.
how_it_works: ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ, ScaNN, Annoy) cluster or graph-structure vectors during indexing so that at query time you don't visit every single one. You start from an entry point, follow local connections toward the query region, and stop early once you've found likely candidates—never scanning the whole dataset. The trade-off is controllable: smaller search radius = faster but lower recall; larger radius = slower but more accurate. Typical latency drops from milliseconds (exact) to microseconds (ANN) on million-scale datasets.
why_it_matters: Vector databases only exist because ANN makes them fast enough to be useful. Dense retrieval in production—embedding search, semantic RAG, similarity matching—would be too slow without it. This speed difference is the gap between a prototype and a real product.
example: A document search system stores 10 million embeddings. Exact search takes 5 seconds per query; HNSW reduces it to 50 milliseconds with 98% recall@10. A team uses FAISS with IVF to shard the index, splitting 100M vectors across 1000 clusters, then queries only 10 clusters per request. Latency stays under 100ms even as the corpus grows.
pitfalls: Tuning recall vs. latency requires knowing your dataset distribution and query patterns; guessing leads to either slow or wrong results.
Index building time scales with dataset size; rebuilding after adding vectors can block production if you don't plan for it.
Recall degrades predictably but not uniformly across query types; rare or outlier queries may miss their best neighbors.
Parameter choices (cluster count, graph connectivity, quantization bits) are dataset-specific; settings that work for text embeddings often fail for images or sparse data.
when_use: Use ANN whenever you need sub-100ms latency on vector datasets larger than 100K or when serving concurrent queries where exact search would saturate CPU.
when_avoid: Avoid ANN if your dataset is small (≤10K vectors), latency is not a bottleneck, or you need mathematically guaranteed top-k results.

faq.q: How much accuracy do I lose with approximate nearest neighbor?
faq.a: Recall@10 typically ranges from 95–99% depending on algorithm and parameters. You miss 1–5% of the true top-10 neighbors but gain 10–100× speed. Tune based on your use case: recommendation engines often tolerate 95% recall; RAG retrieval may require 99%+.

faq.q: Is HNSW better than IVF?
faq.a: HNSW usually offers higher recall at lower latency but requires more memory and slower indexing. IVF indexes faster and uses less RAM, making it better for frequently-updated datasets. Choice depends on your read/write ratio and memory budget.

faq.q: Can I use approximate nearest neighbor for real-time updates?
faq.a: Some algorithms support incremental insertion (HNSW, certain IVF variants), but full index rebuilds are still common. If you need to add millions of vectors daily, plan for offline indexing or accept slightly stale indexes.

faq.q: What's the difference between approximate nearest neighbor and semantic caching?
faq.a: ANN finds similar vectors in a large index; semantic caching checks if a new query is similar to past queries you've already cached. They solve different problems: ANN speeds up retrieval, caching avoids duplicate computation.

faq.q: Does approximate nearest neighbor work for high-dimensional vectors?
faq.a: Yes, but performance degrades as dimensionality increases (curse of dimensionality). Dimensionality reduction or quantization (compressing vectors to fewer bits) helps mitigate this on 1000+ dimensional embeddings.

title: Bi-Encoder
tldr: Two-tower architecture that encodes queries and documents separately into vectors, enabling fast indexed retrieval.
plain_english: A bi-encoder runs a query and a document through the same neural network (or twin networks) independently, each producing a single fixed-size vector. You then measure relevance by comparing how similar those vectors are—usually with cosine distance or dot product. Because document vectors are pre-computed and indexed, retrieval is fast; the tradeoff is that the model never sees query and document together, so it can't model deep interaction patterns.
how_it_works: You encode all documents once offline, storing their vectors in a database (FAISS, HNSW, or similar). At query time, you encode the incoming query using the same encoder, then find the top-k nearest vectors via approximate nearest neighbor search. Relevance scores are derived from vector similarity. Document vectors stay frozen until reindexing; query encoding is the only per-request computation. This separation is why bi-encoders scale to billions of documents—you trade ranking precision for retrieval speed.
why_it_matters: Bi-encoders power dense retrieval in almost every production RAG system. They sit at the speed–quality frontier: much faster than cross-encoders for initial retrieval, fast enough to index massive corpora, yet accurate enough for modern search. Understanding when bi-encoders break down (low-resource languages, domain-specific relevance signals) is critical for designing effective retrieval pipelines.
example: A document search system at a large enterprise might index 10M internal wiki pages with BGE-Large offline, storing vectors in FAISS. When a user types a query, the system encodes it with the same model and retrieves the top-100 candidates in ~50ms. A cross-encoder re-ranks those 100 for final precision, avoiding the cost of scoring all 10M documents. This hybrid approach balances speed and accuracy.
pitfalls: Bi-encoders cannot model query-document interactions; use a reranker for high-precision ranking of initial results.
Indexing lag: updating document embeddings requires full recompute and reindex; not suitable for real-time document ingestion without careful batching.
Poor performance on relevance signals that require context (e.g., 'find docs contradicting my query')—cross-encoders excel here.
Vocabulary mismatch and paraphrase sensitivity can hurt retrieval if your queries use different language than your documents.
when_use: Use bi-encoders for initial retrieval in RAG when you need sub-second latency over millions of documents and can afford a second-stage reranker for precision.
when_avoid: Avoid bi-encoders alone for high-precision ranking tasks or when query-document semantic interaction is critical to relevance; pair with a cross-encoder instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between bi-encoder and cross-encoder?
faq.a: A bi-encoder encodes query and document separately and compares their vectors; a cross-encoder feeds both together into the model and outputs a single relevance score. Bi-encoders are faster; cross-encoders are more accurate for ranking.

faq.q: Can I use a bi-encoder for real-time retrieval?
faq.a: Yes, query encoding is fast (~10–50ms). Document indexing is offline, so if documents change infrequently, real-time retrieval works. High-velocity document streams require careful batch-reindexing strategy.

faq.q: Is a bi-encoder the same as an embedding model?
faq.a: Not exactly. An embedding model is a general term for any encoder that produces vectors. A bi-encoder is a specific architecture using two independent encodings. All bi-encoders are embedding models, but not all embedding models are bi-encoders.

faq.q: Why do people use bi-encoders if cross-encoders are more accurate?
faq.a: Speed. Bi-encoders precompute document vectors once; cross-encoders must score every candidate per query. For 1M+ documents, bi-encoder retrieval + cross-encoder reranking is faster than cross-encoder-only scoring.

faq.q: Can I combine bi-encoder retrieval with a reranker?
faq.a: Yes—this is the standard practice in production RAG. Bi-encoder retrieves top-k candidates fast, then a cross-encoder or LLM reranks them for final precision.

title: BM25
tldr: Sparse retrieval algorithm scoring documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and length normalization.
plain_english: BM25 treats search like counting: documents get higher scores when they match query terms frequently, when those terms are rare across all documents, and when the document length is proportional to its content. Think of it as a statistical ranking system that avoids over-rewarding long documents or common words. It's been the default retrieval method in search engines for decades because it works predictably across different domains.
how_it_works: For each query term, BM25 calculates a score based on three factors: how often the term appears in a document (term frequency, capped by parameter k1 to prevent diminishing returns), how rare the term is across all documents (inverse document frequency), and how the document's length compares to average length (controlled by parameter b). The final document score is the sum of these per-term contributions. Typical values are k1=1.2 and b=0.75, tuned empirically for your domain. You don't train BM25—you compute it directly from term counts and corpus statistics.
why_it_matters: BM25 is the battle-tested retrieval baseline. Most modern RAG systems pair it with embeddings in hybrid search: BM25 catches exact and rare-term matches, while embeddings find semantic neighbors. BM25 requires no model training, stays fast at scale, and interprets easily—making it ideal for systems where you need both performance and debugging visibility.
example: A customer support platform indexes 100k FAQ documents. When someone searches 'reset password after two-factor auth', BM25 ranks documents high if they contain all three terms ('reset', 'password', 'two-factor') and penalizes generic onboarding guides that mention 'password' everywhere. The system re-ranks top-20 BM25 results with a cross-encoder, cutting latency versus pure embedding retrieval while catching exact-match questions the embeddings might miss.
pitfalls: Ignoring document structure: BM25 scores raw text, so PDFs parsed as one 50-page field lose positional signals; chunk and index smaller units.
Tuning k1 and b without a held-out test set; defaults work well but corpus-specific tuning requires labeled examples of good vs bad rankings.
Assuming BM25 handles synonyms: 'login' and 'sign in' are different terms; pair with embeddings or synonym expansion.
Over-relying on BM25 for semantic queries like 'How do I recover from failure?' where exact keywords don't appear—embeddings outperform.
when_use: Use BM25 when you need fast, interpretable retrieval for keyword-heavy queries, have limited labeled data for tuning embeddings, or want a baseline leg of hybrid search.
when_avoid: Avoid BM25 alone for semantic or synonym-heavy queries, when documents are very long and you need fine-grained positional matching, or when your query vocabulary differs significantly from document vocabulary.

faq.q: How is BM25 different from TF-IDF?
faq.a: BM25 extends TF-IDF with saturation (term frequency stops increasing at k1) and explicit length normalization (parameter b). This prevents long documents from dominating rankings and makes scores more stable across document collections.

faq.q: Should I use BM25 or embeddings?
faq.a: Neither—use both. BM25 excels at exact and rare-term matches; embeddings find semantic neighbors. Hybrid search ranks by combining both signals, then re-ranks with a cross-encoder for best results.

faq.q: What are k1 and b parameters and how do I tune them?
faq.a: k1 (default 1.2) controls how much term frequency matters—higher values reward repeated terms more. b (default 0.75) controls length normalization—0 disables it, 1 normalizes fully. Tune both on a labeled test set of query-document pairs you've marked as relevant or irrelevant.

faq.q: Can I use BM25 for non-English text?
faq.a: Yes, but results depend on tokenization and stemming. BM25 is language-agnostic; the quality comes from how you preprocess—proper stemming or lemmatization for your language matters more than the algorithm itself.

faq.q: Does BM25 work for very long documents?
faq.a: It handles them, but with caveats. Parameter b normalizes length, so a 10-page document isn't unfairly boosted. However, splitting into smaller chunks often improves ranking because queries match discrete topics better than whole documents.

title: Chunking (Document Chunking)
tldr: Split documents into smaller pieces before embedding. Chunk size and boundaries directly control RAG retrieval quality.
plain_english: Your embedding model has a token limit (often 512–8192 tokens). Documents are longer. Chunking decides how to break them into pieces the embedder can handle. Think of it like how you'd split a long article into paragraphs—too small and each piece loses context, too large and you waste retrieval on irrelevant sections.
how_it_works: Fixed-size chunking splits documents at a token boundary (e.g., 512 tokens with 50-token overlap). Sentence-aware splitting respects paragraph and section breaks to preserve semantic coherence. Semantic chunking uses embeddings to detect where meaning shifts, then splits there. Each approach trades simplicity for retrieval precision. Chunk overlap prevents information loss at boundaries, but does not eliminate cross-chunk references. Typical range: 300–800 tokens per chunk with 50–100 token overlap.
why_it_matters: Chunking quality determines what the retriever can find. Bad chunks mean bad retrieval, which breaks everything downstream—no embedder or reranker fix it. Most RAG performance issues trace to chunking decisions made in the first week of a project, yet they're often treated as an afterthought.
example: A team building a customer support RAG on a 500-page product manual tested three approaches: fixed 256-token chunks (missed nuance), fixed 1024-token chunks (retrieved irrelevant sections), and semantic chunking at meaning boundaries (22% better retrieval hit rate). The semantic approach cost 3× more embedding calls but reduced reranking load and improved answer quality measurably.
pitfalls: Chunking by character count instead of tokens—500 characters ≈ 125 tokens in English but 350 in Chinese; the embedder cares about tokens, not characters.
Assuming overlap prevents all boundary loss—cross-paragraph references and dependent sentences still break when split at fixed offsets.
Using the same chunk size for all documents—highly structured documents (code, JSON) need different boundaries than narrative prose.
Ignoring metadata during chunking—you lose document structure, headers, and section context that improve retrieval ranking.
when_use: Use chunking whenever you build a RAG system. Adjust size and strategy based on document type: semantic chunking for prose, sentence-aware for mixed content, structured splits for code or tables.
when_avoid: Avoid chunking when you're not retrieving—if you embed entire documents and retrieve whole documents, chunking adds no value and wastes compute.

faq.q: What's the difference between chunking and tokenization?
faq.a: Tokenization breaks text into individual tokens (words, subwords). Chunking groups many tokens into logical pieces for embedding and retrieval. Tokenization is a step within chunking.

faq.q: How do I choose chunk size?
faq.a: Start at 300–500 tokens for dense retrieval (BERT-like models) or 512–1024 for larger models. Test on a holdout set and adjust if retrieval accuracy drops or noise increases. Document type matters: code needs smaller chunks than narrative.

faq.q: Does chunk overlap really prevent information loss?
faq.a: Overlap reduces loss at chunk boundaries but does not eliminate it. A sentence split across two chunks may lose context. Semantic or sentence-aware chunking is more reliable than overlap alone.

faq.q: Can I use the same chunk size for all my documents?
faq.a: No. Structured data (code, JSON, tables) needs smaller, syntax-aware chunks. Narrative prose tolerates larger chunks. Mixed documents need multiple strategies or post-processing to preserve structure.

faq.q: Should I chunk before or after removing stop words?
faq.a: Chunk first, on raw text. Stop-word removal is a preprocessing step for sparse retrieval (BM25), not dense retrieval (embeddings). For RAG, preserve original text during chunking to maintain meaning.

title: ColBERT (Late Interaction Retrieval)
tldr: Retrieval model that scores documents by matching individual query tokens to document tokens, balancing speed and ranking quality.
plain_english: ColBERT treats your query and documents as bags of individual word vectors rather than single whole-document vectors. Instead of comparing one query vector to one document vector (like a bi-encoder does), it compares each query word to every document word and picks the best match for each query word. This gives you cross-encoder-like ranking quality at a fraction of the computational cost.
how_it_works: ColBERT encodes both query and document tokens separately into contextualized vectors using BERT. At scoring time, for each query token, it finds the maximum similarity to any document token (MaxSim operation) and sums these maximums across all query tokens. This late interaction design—computing similarities only at retrieval time, not at indexing time—lets you index once and reuse embeddings for many queries. ColBERTv2 adds quantization (compressing embeddings to 1–2 bytes) and PLAID adds learned sparse retrieval patterns to speed up token matching.
why_it_matters: You get cross-encoder-level ranking quality (higher precision for reranking) at 100× lower inference cost than joint encoding. This makes it practical to retrieve and rank thousands of candidates in production without hitting latency budgets or GPU limits.
example: A customer-support chatbot at a mid-size SaaS firm indexes 50,000 help articles once with ColBERT. When a user asks a question, the system retrieves top-20 candidate articles in ~50ms, then re-scores them with ColBERT in ~10ms more. A bi-encoder would need 5–10ms to retrieve, but ranking quality would be mediocre; a cross-encoder would rank perfectly but take 200ms+ per query. ColBERT splits the difference.
pitfalls: Token-level matching means multi-token synonyms or paraphrases don't match well unless tokens overlap semantically—query 'vehicle' won't necessarily find docs saying 'automobile' unless training covered that pair.
Memory footprint grows with document length since you store a vector for every token, not one per document. Long documents or large collections can exhaust GPU memory during batch retrieval.
MaxSim scores are not calibrated across different document lengths; longer docs tend to score higher simply because more tokens offer more chances for a good match.
Index compression (ColBERTv2) trades off some ranking quality for speed and disk space. You must tune compression aggressively vs. being conservative based on your precision vs. latency tradeoff.
when_use: Use ColBERT when you need to rank hundreds or thousands of candidates with high precision in real-time and your budget doesn't allow per-query cross-encoder inference.
when_avoid: Avoid ColBERT if you have very few candidate documents (< 100) per query—a simple cross-encoder is simpler and sufficient. Also avoid if your queries and docs have little token-level vocabulary overlap, or if you cannot store token-level embeddings in memory.

faq.q: How is ColBERT different from a bi-encoder?
faq.a: A bi-encoder computes one vector per document and one per query, then ranks via single dot product. ColBERT computes a vector per token, then ranks by matching tokens individually. ColBERT ranks higher quality but costs more at retrieval time.

faq.q: Can ColBERT replace a cross-encoder?
faq.a: Not exactly. Cross-encoders are more accurate when you have few candidates (< 100). ColBERT is faster and more practical at scale. Many pipelines use ColBERT to retrieve hundreds, then a cross-encoder to rerank the top 10–20.

faq.q: Does ColBERT require GPU to run?
faq.a: Not mandatory, but GPU makes token matching fast. CPU inference is possible for small collections but will be slow. Most production setups use GPU or quantized embeddings (ColBERTv2) to keep latency under 100ms.

faq.q: How much memory does indexing with ColBERT use?
faq.a: Memory scales with total number of tokens across all documents. A 1M-document corpus with average 150 tokens per doc = ~150M tokens. At 128 dims and 4 bytes per float, that's ~80 GB before compression. ColBERTv2 quantization reduces this to ~1–2 GB.

faq.q: What does late interaction mean in ColBERT?
faq.a: Late interaction means you compute token similarities only at retrieval time, not during indexing. You store all token embeddings and match them on demand, rather than precomputing query-document scores. This lets you reuse the index for different queries.

title: Contextual Compression (Retrieval)
tldr: Filter retrieved documents through an LLM to extract only sentences answering your query before passing to the generator.
plain_english: When you search a database, you often get back chunks that are 80% noise around 20% useful signal. Contextual compression runs those chunks through a lightweight LLM or classifier that pulls out only the relevant sentences, like a highlighter pen on paper. You send the compressed excerpt to your final answer-generator instead of the bloated original.
how_it_works: After retrieval returns candidate chunks, each chunk flows through a compressor—either a small LLM (like a 7B model) or a learned token classifier. The compressor extracts spans (sentences or phrases) that directly address your query and discards the rest. The filtered excerpts, now 40–60% of original token length, feed into your final generator. LangChain implements this via ContextualCompressionRetriever with LLMExtractor. The compressor sees the query and chunk in context, so it makes relevance decisions without losing semantic coherence.
why_it_matters: Compression cuts generator token cost by 40–60% while often improving answer quality by removing distracting context. In large-corpus RAG systems, this directly reduces latency and expense per query. It's become a standard optimization layer for production retrieval pipelines.
example: A customer support team indexes 50,000 knowledge-base articles. A user query returns 10 chunks averaging 800 tokens each. Without compression, the generator sees 8,000 tokens of mixed signal and noise. With compression, each chunk gets distilled to 200–300 relevant tokens, cutting input to ~2,500 tokens total. The generator produces faster, more focused answers, and support latency drops by 2 seconds per ticket.
pitfalls: Compression can truncate nuanced context—a sentence that looks irrelevant in isolation may be critical for reasoning; test with domain-specific queries.
Over-aggressive compression using weak models (tiny classifiers) removes signal and hurts answer quality; verify F1 scores on held-out query-document pairs.
Compressor latency compounds with large retrieval batches; monitor end-to-end inference time, not just generator time.
If your compressor is an LLM, it adds cost per chunk; smaller models or token classifiers are cheaper but need labeled training data.
when_use: Use contextual compression when retrieved chunks average >400 tokens, your generator has tight token or latency budgets, or you're retrieving from long documents (PDFs, wikis, transcripts).
when_avoid: Avoid compression if chunks are already <200 tokens, your domain requires full context for reasoning, or your compressor has higher latency than your generator savings justify.

faq.q: How is contextual compression different from reranking?
faq.a: Reranking sorts chunks by relevance score; compression extracts relevant spans within each chunk. Reranking picks which chunks to keep; compression extracts what within them matters. Both reduce noise, but compression is more granular.

faq.q: What model should I use to compress?
faq.a: Use a lightweight LLM (7B–13B) if token budget allows, or train a learned token classifier on labeled query-chunk-span triples if you have clean data. For quick prototyping, any LLM works; for production, smaller models or classifiers are faster and cheaper.

faq.q: Does compression hurt answer quality?
faq.a: Not if tuned carefully. Compression often improves quality by removing distractors, but weak compressors can strip critical context. Validate on your domain's queries and documents before deploying.

faq.q: Can I combine compression with other retrieval steps?
faq.a: Yes. Common stacks are: retrieve → rerank → compress → generate. Reranking filters which chunks; compression refines what stays in each chunk. Order matters—usually rerank first (cheaper, bulk filter), then compress (finer-grained).

faq.q: What's the typical token reduction?
faq.a: Contextual compression typically reduces token count by 40–70% depending on document density and query specificity. You trade some compressor latency (~100–300ms for an LLM per chunk) for generator savings.

mainEntity.name: How is contextual compression different from reranking?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reranking sorts chunks by relevance score; compression extracts relevant spans within each chunk. Reranking picks which chunks to keep; compression extracts what within them matters. Both reduce noise, but compression is more granular.

mainEntity.name: What model should I use to compress?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a lightweight LLM (7B–13B) if token budget allows, or train a learned token classifier on labeled query-chunk-span triples if you have clean data. For quick prototyping, any LLM works; for production, smaller models or classifiers are faster and cheaper.

mainEntity.name: Does compression hurt answer quality?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not if tuned carefully. Compression often improves quality by removing distractors, but weak compressors can strip critical context. Validate on your domain's queries and documents before deploying.

mainEntity.name: Can I combine compression with other retrieval steps?

mainEntity.name: What's the typical token reduction?

acceptedAnswer.text: Contextual compression typically reduces token count by 40–70% depending on document density and query specificity. You trade some compressor latency (~100–300ms for an LLM per chunk) for generator savings.

title: Cosine Similarity
tldr: Measures angle between embedding vectors; ignores magnitude. Ranges -1 to 1; 0.8+ signals semantic relevance.
plain_english: Cosine similarity asks: "Do these two vectors point in the same direction?" Think of it like comparing compass headings. Two people walking toward different destinations can still walk in nearly the same direction. It's the standard metric for vector search because it cares about direction, not how far from origin each vector sits.
how_it_works: Given vectors A and B, cosine similarity = (A · B) / (|A| × |B|). Most embedding models output normalized vectors (length = 1), so this reduces to just the dot product—computationally cheap. Output ranges from -1 (opposite directions) to 1 (identical). In practice: 0.8+ typically indicates semantically related content; >0.9 suggests near-duplicate. The metric is invariant to magnitude: scaling a vector doesn't change its cosine to another vector.
why_it_matters: Cosine similarity powers every vector search in RAG and semantic retrieval. The difference between a 0.75 and 0.85 threshold changes what users find: one catches "related" documents, the other surfaces "near-duplicates." Using the wrong threshold degrades retrieval quality or returns false positives that waste reranking budget.
example: A customer-support RAG system indexes 50k help articles as embeddings. When a user asks "How do I reset my password?", the system ranks all articles by cosine similarity. Articles with 0.88+ similarity ("Password reset steps", "Account recovery") surface; those with 0.72 similarity ("Two-factor authentication") are ranked lower. Setting threshold at 0.82 instead of 0.75 cuts noise by 40% but risks missing valid edge cases.
pitfalls: High cosine similarity does not guarantee semantic equivalence: 'I love cats' and 'I hate cats' often score ~0.85+ because word order and sentence structure dominate the signal.
Cosine similarity only makes sense if your embedding model was trained with a cosine or contrastive objective; using it on models trained with L2 loss can degrade recall.
Normalized vectors are assumed in all fast implementations; verify your embeddings are unit-length or normalize before computing.
A cosine of 0 does not mean unrelated—it means orthogonal; near-zero values for text embeddings are actually rare and often indicate sparse or malformed vectors.
when_use: Use cosine similarity when comparing embeddings from the same model, especially for vector search, semantic similarity ranking, or clustering tasks where direction matters more than magnitude.
when_avoid: Avoid cosine similarity when comparing embeddings from different model architectures, when your embedding model was trained without a cosine objective, or when magnitude itself carries semantic meaning (e.g., confidence scores).

faq.q: What's the difference between cosine similarity and Euclidean distance?
faq.a: Cosine similarity measures angle (direction); Euclidean distance measures straight-line magnitude. For normalized embeddings, cosine is invariant to vector length, making it more reliable for text. Euclidean distance favors vectors closer to the origin, which can distort retrieval.

faq.q: When should I use a 0.8 vs 0.9 threshold?
faq.a: Use 0.9+ for high-precision retrieval (near-duplicates, exact-match intent). Use 0.75–0.85 for high-recall (catch all related documents). Your choice depends on whether you'd rather miss relevant results or waste reranking on borderline matches.

faq.q: Does cosine similarity work with all embedding models?
faq.a: It works best with models trained with cosine or contrastive loss (BERT, Sentence Transformers, modern text-embedding models). It can fail silently on models trained with L2 loss, where dot product may be more reliable.

faq.q: Why is cosine similarity used in RAG instead of dot product?
faq.a: They're equivalent if embeddings are normalized. Cosine is preferred because it's invariant to vector magnitude—two embeddings pointing the same way are similar regardless of length. This makes thresholds more interpretable across different inputs.

faq.q: Can cosine similarity detect near-duplicates?
faq.a: Yes, but imperfectly. True duplicates score >0.95–0.99. Near-duplicates (same content, different wording) typically score 0.85–0.95. Use rerankers for nuanced tasks; cosine alone conflates surface-level similarity with semantic equivalence.

title: Cross-Encoder
tldr: A model that scores query-document pairs together for high-accuracy reranking, at higher computational cost.
plain_english: A cross-encoder is like a referee that reads both your question and a candidate answer side-by-side, then rates how well they match. Unlike models that score documents in isolation, cross-encoders let query and document tokens interact directly, producing more accurate relevance scores. The tradeoff: you need one forward pass per document, so they're slow for large candidate sets.
how_it_works: You concatenate a query and a document into a single input sequence and pass both through a shared transformer. Query and document tokens attend to each other at every layer, building mutual context. A classification or regression head on top outputs a relevance score. At inference time, you run this process once per candidate document—if you have 50 candidates, that's 50 forward passes. This O(k·doc_len) cost is why cross-encoders are used only to rerank a small set (10–100 docs) already retrieved by a cheaper bi-encoder or sparse method.
why_it_matters: Cross-encoders close the accuracy gap that pure dense retrieval leaves open. Stacking them atop bi-encoder retrieval is standard RAG practice: retrieve ~100 candidates cheaply, rerank with a cross-encoder to get the top 5–10. This two-stage pipeline balances speed and relevance better than either method alone.
example: A customer support RAG system retrieves 50 candidate FAQ answers using BM25, then passes each (user_question, faq_answer) pair through a cross-encoder like mxbai-rerank-xlarge. The cross-encoder assigns a score to each pair; the top 3 are passed to the LLM for final response generation. This two-stage approach cuts token cost compared to reranking all 50 with the LLM directly.
pitfalls: Treating cross-encoders as standalone retrievers—they need a large candidate pool from a first-stage ranker to be cost-effective.
Using cross-encoders on all documents at indexing time instead of query time; the cost scales linearly with corpus size, not query count.
Forgetting that cross-encoder scores are not normalized across different query-document pair distributions; don't compare raw scores across unrelated queries.
Assuming longer documents always benefit from cross-encoder reranking; very long docs may bottleneck the forward pass and add minimal ranking signal.
when_use: Use a cross-encoder when you need high-precision reranking of 10–200 pre-filtered candidates and latency or cost from a second LLM call is acceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid cross-encoders for initial retrieval over large corpora, real-time systems with <100ms latency budgets, or when a single-stage bi-encoder achieves your recall target.

faq.q: What's the difference between a cross-encoder and a bi-encoder?
faq.a: A bi-encoder scores query and document separately and compares embeddings; a cross-encoder scores them together in a single forward pass. Cross-encoders are more accurate but slower per document.

faq.q: Can I use a cross-encoder to retrieve documents from a large corpus?
faq.a: Technically yes, but it's prohibitively expensive—you'd score every document at query time. Use a bi-encoder or BM25 for initial retrieval, then cross-encoder to rerank the top candidates.

faq.q: How much faster is a cross-encoder compared to using an LLM to rerank?
faq.a: Cross-encoders are 10–100× faster and cheaper per ranking decision than querying an LLM, making them practical for reranking 50+ candidates in real time.

faq.q: Do cross-encoders work for ranking beyond document relevance?
faq.a: Yes. Any ranking task benefit from direct token interaction—relevance, semantic similarity, question-answer alignment, or query-passage pairing.

title: Dense Retrieval
tldr: Retrieval that embeds queries and documents into vectors, then finds matches by similarity distance in that shared space.
plain_english: Dense retrieval converts both your question and stored documents into lists of numbers (vectors) in a shared space, then finds the closest ones mathematically. Think of it like plotting everything on a map where semantically similar things naturally cluster together. Unlike keyword matching, it understands paraphrases and synonyms because it's measuring meaning, not exact words.
how_it_works: An embedding model (often a fine-tuned transformer) projects queries and documents into high-dimensional vectors. At search time, your query gets embedded, and an approximate nearest neighbor index (HNSW, IVF-PQ) quickly locates the vectors closest in cosine similarity or dot product. The retrieved documents are ranked by that distance score. Modern systems index billions of documents this way by compressing vectors into shorter codes or using specialized hardware.
why_it_matters: Dense retrieval handles semantic matching where sparse methods fail—paraphrased questions, synonyms, and conceptual overlap. It's now the default RAG backbone because it scales well and matches human intent better than keyword lookup. For teams building search over domain documents, dense retrieval cuts through vocabulary mismatches.
example: A customer support team uses dense retrieval to find relevant knowledge articles when users ask variations like 'How do I reset my password?' and 'I forgot my login code'—synonyms BM25 would miss. By embedding both articles and incoming questions, the system returns the right article even when wording differs, reducing false-negatives and improving first-contact resolution.
pitfalls: Dense retrieval performs worse on rare named entities, product codes, and exact-match queries where sparse methods excel—always hybrid-search instead of replacing sparse entirely.
Embedding quality depends entirely on your model choice; a generic BERT embedding won't match finance jargon the way a domain-tuned model will.
ANN indexes trade accuracy for speed; approximate misses happen, especially at scale—measure recall on your real queries, not just benchmarks.
Late interaction (cross-encoder reranking) is needed to re-rank dense results reliably; dense alone often has lower precision than you'd expect.
when_use: Use dense retrieval when queries are paraphrased, conceptual, or involve synonyms, and when you can afford embedding compute and storage overhead.
when_avoid: Avoid dense retrieval alone if your queries are keyword-heavy, contain rare entities, or need guaranteed exact-match recall; pair with sparse or use sparse exclusively for those cases.

faq.q: Is dense retrieval better than BM25?
faq.a: Not universally. Dense handles paraphrases and synonyms well; BM25 wins on rare terms and exact matches. Use both in hybrid search for the best of each.

faq.q: What embedding model should I use for dense retrieval?
faq.a: Start with a general model (BGE, nomic-embed, e5) unless your domain is specialized (finance, biomedical). Fine-tune on your own data if generic embeddings don't capture your jargon.

faq.q: How much does dense retrieval slow down search compared to keyword search?
faq.a: Embedding a query is milliseconds; ANN lookup is also fast. Bottleneck is often the embedding model itself and network latency to your index, not the algorithm.

faq.q: Can I use dense retrieval on a billion documents?
faq.a: Yes, with product quantization or IVF-PQ to compress vectors, and distributed systems like FAISS on GPUs. Trade-off: compression reduces recall slightly.

faq.q: Do I need to reindex documents if I change embedding models?
faq.a: Yes—vectors from different models are incompatible. Plan for offline reindexing or shadow deploy the new model before swapping it in production.

title: FAISS
tldr: Meta's open-source library for fast nearest-neighbor search on dense vectors using optimized CPU and GPU indexes.
plain_english: FAISS lets you search through millions of vectors (embeddings) much faster than brute-force comparison. Think of it like a library card catalog that groups similar books together, so you don't have to check every single book to find what you need. You give it vectors, build an index structure once, then query it to find the closest matches in milliseconds.
how_it_works: You feed FAISS a batch of vectors and choose an index type—IVF (Inverted File), HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), or PQ (Product Quantization) are common choices. Each trades search speed against memory use and accuracy. FAISS builds the index, storing vectors in a structured format on CPU or GPU. At query time, you pass a vector and get back the k nearest neighbors ranked by similarity. The library provides optimized kernels for both CPU and NVIDIA/AMD GPUs, making it practical for billions of vectors.
why_it_matters: Vector search is the bottleneck in RAG, semantic search, and recommendation systems. FAISS is the reference implementation that underlies most vector databases (Milvus, LanceDB, Pinecone's internals). Understanding its index trade-offs—speed, memory, accuracy—helps you choose the right database and tune retrieval for your latency budget.
example: A content team at a news org embeds 10M articles into 384-dim vectors. Using brute-force similarity search would take 2–5 seconds per query. With FAISS's IVF index on CPU, they get results in 50ms, making real-time "related stories" feasible. Switching to GPU hardware with the same index cuts that to 5–10ms under load.
pitfalls: Confusing FAISS with a vector database: FAISS is an index library, not a storage or query engine—you manage the data yourself.
Building a single huge index without partitioning: FAISS scales linearly with dataset size; for billions of vectors, you need sharding across machines.
Ignoring the accuracy-speed tradeoff: IVF is 10x faster than HNSW but needs tuning (nprobe parameter) to avoid missing top results.
Using GPU indexes without measuring end-to-end latency: GPU data transfer overhead can negate index speedup for small batches or frequent rebuilds.
when_use: Use FAISS when you own your vectors, need sub-100ms latency on millions of embeddings, and can tolerate building and managing indexes yourself or when you want to understand how vector search works under the hood.
when_avoid: Avoid FAISS if you need automatic rebalancing on changing data, multi-tenancy at scale, or schema flexibility—use a managed vector database instead. Also avoid if your dataset is small (<100K vectors) where brute-force is fast enough.

faq.q: Is FAISS a vector database?
faq.a: No. FAISS is a search library for in-memory indexes. It doesn't store data persistently or handle updates—you manage the vectors and rebuild indexes as needed. Vector databases like Milvus and LanceDB often use FAISS as their index engine.

faq.q: How do I choose between IVF, HNSW, and PQ in FAISS?
faq.a: IVF is fastest for large datasets and GPU; HNSW has better accuracy with fewer tuning knobs; PQ saves memory for very large embeddings. Start with IVF for RAG (tuning nprobe), switch to HNSW if accuracy degrades and latency allows.

faq.q: Can FAISS handle real-time data updates?
faq.a: FAISS is not designed for frequent updates. You either rebuild indexes offline or use incremental index types (IDMap), which are slower. For continuously changing data, use a vector database that handles updates natively.

faq.q: What's the maximum dataset size FAISS can handle?
faq.a: A single FAISS process can handle ~100M to 1B vectors depending on index type and hardware. Beyond that, shard vectors across machines and maintain separate FAISS indexes, then merge results (reducing recall at scale).

faq.q: Does FAISS require GPU?
faq.a: No, FAISS works on CPU, but GPU (NVIDIA/AMD) accelerates search 5–20x depending on index type and batch size. GPU is optional and most useful for high-throughput serving.

title: Graph RAG
tldr: RAG system that extracts entities and relationships from documents, builds a knowledge graph, and traverses connections to answer multi-hop questions.
plain_english: Instead of treating documents as isolated chunks, Graph RAG first identifies the people, places, and concepts in them—and how they relate to each other—then maps those connections like a social network. At query time, it travels along those relationship paths to pull together context spread across multiple documents. This approach works much better when you need to answer questions like "how are X and Y related?" rather than simple lookup questions.
how_it_works: Graph RAG extracts entities and relations from source documents using an LLM or relation extractor, then builds a directed graph where nodes are entities and edges are relationships. The graph is clustered into communities, and summaries are generated per community to reduce noise. At query time, the system traverses the graph to identify connected subgraphs relevant to the question, retrieves the corresponding community summaries or chunks, and sends that context to the LLM for answer generation. This multi-hop traversal captures dependencies that chunk-based RAG would miss—for example, finding that Person A invested in Company B, which later hired Person C.
why_it_matters: On knowledge-heavy domains with implicit multi-hop relationships, Graph RAG significantly outperforms vanilla retrieval-augmented generation. It reduces hallucination by grounding answers in explicitly mapped connections and handles questions that require reasoning across document boundaries. The tradeoff is higher upfront indexing cost and slower query latency, making it most valuable for stable, relation-rich corpora like research literature, financial networks, or organizational knowledge bases.
example: A venture capital analyst uses Graph RAG to index 500 company profiles, founder bios, and investment rounds. When asked "Which founders have backed companies in the AI infrastructure space?", the system traverses from founder nodes → invested-in edges → company nodes → sector tags, retrieving all matching paths in seconds. A simpler RAG would only find chunks mentioning both terms together, missing indirect relationships.
pitfalls: Graph construction is expensive—extraction errors compound if entities are mis-identified or relationships are hallucinated; validate extraction quality on a sample before indexing at scale.
Community clustering quality directly affects retrieval; poorly chosen cluster sizes can group unrelated concepts or fragment relevant ones; requires tuning per domain.
Query latency increases with graph size; traversal can be slow on million-node graphs without indexing optimizations like approximate nearest neighbor search on embeddings.

Relation extraction from unstructured text remains brittle; the system will miss implicit relationships not explicitly mentioned (e.g., "Person A reported to Person B" vs "Person B supervised Person A").
when_use: Use Graph RAG when your corpus contains explicit relationships between entities, you regularly field multi-hop or connection-based questions, or you have a stable, well-defined schema where entities and relation types repeat.
when_avoid: Avoid Graph RAG for rapid-changing documents, when most questions are simple lookups or keyword searches, or when relation extraction cost exceeds the benefit of improved accuracy.

faq.q: Is Graph RAG the same as knowledge graph retrieval?
faq.a: Related but distinct. A knowledge graph is the data structure; Graph RAG is a retrieval pattern that builds and queries a knowledge graph specifically for answering questions. You can build a knowledge graph without using RAG, and you can use RAG without a graph.

faq.q: How much faster is Graph RAG than regular RAG?
faq.a: On multi-hop questions, Graph RAG often returns more relevant context per query. Wall-clock latency depends on graph size and indexing—traversal can add 100–500ms per query, offset by needing fewer LLM calls if context is more precise.

faq.q: Can Graph RAG work without an LLM for extraction?
faq.a: Yes, you can use rule-based extractors, NER models, or dependency parsers; however, LLM-based extraction is more flexible for domain-specific relations. The tradeoff is cost and latency during indexing.

faq.q: What's the minimum corpus size for Graph RAG to be worth it?
faq.a: Below ~100 interconnected documents with clear entity relationships, the overhead of graph construction often outweighs the retrieval benefit. Start with regular RAG and add a graph layer if you see patterns of missed connections.

faq.q: Does Graph RAG hallucinate less than regular RAG?
faq.a: Not inherently—hallucination depends on the LLM and prompt. Graph RAG reduces hallucination by providing more grounded context via explicit relationships, but a poor extraction model can introduce false relations that propagate downstream.

title: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)
tldr: Multi-layer graph algorithm that finds nearest vectors in log(N) time instead of scanning all vectors.
plain_english: Imagine searching a phonebook by first narrowing to the right region, then the right town, then the right street. HNSW builds a multi-layer graph where top layers contain fewer nodes spaced far apart (coarse search) and lower layers contain more nodes packed closer (fine search). Your query starts at the top, hops between distant nodes quickly, then descends to lower layers to pin down the exact nearest neighbors.
how_it_works: HNSW constructs a graph where each vector is a node with connections to its nearest neighbors at multiple scales. During indexing, it assigns nodes to layers probabilistically and links each node to M neighbors per layer (M typically 16–32). At query time, search starts at the topmost layer with few nodes, greedy-walks to a local best, then drops to the next layer and repeats until reaching the bottom layer. Higher M increases memory and recall; higher ef_search (beam width) at query time trades speed for accuracy. The algorithm converges in O(log N) hops instead of comparing against all N vectors.
why_it_matters: HNSW makes searching millions of vectors practical. It's the default indexing strategy in production vector databases (Qdrant, Pinecone, pgvector) because it delivers 50–100× speedup over linear scan while keeping recall above 0.95 with reasonable tuning. Without it, semantic search at scale becomes economically unviable.
example: A team building document search over 50 million customer support tickets indexes them with HNSW (M=32, ef_construct=200) in Qdrant. At query time, a user's question returns top-10 similar tickets in 15ms using ef=128. If they later increase ef=256, latency rises to 25ms but recall improves to 0.99; they tune the tradeoff based on SLA.
pitfalls: HNSW is approximate, not exact — Recall@10 of 0.95 is typical; chasing 0.99 requires significant ef_search tuning and latency cost.
Indexes consume 1.5–2× the raw vector size in memory due to graph pointers; you cannot shrink them when deleting vectors without rebuilding.
M and ef_construct are set at index creation and affect all queries; mistuning for your recall target wastes memory or forces slow queries.
Small M (e.g., 8) saves memory but hurts recall even with high ef_search; the graph becomes too sparse to navigate efficiently.
when_use: Use HNSW when you need to search millions+ of vectors under 100ms latency and can accept 0.95–0.99 recall instead of exact nearest neighbors.
when_avoid: Avoid HNSW if your corpus is under 10K vectors (linear scan is sufficient) or if you need 100% exact recall (use brute-force or LSH variants).

faq.q: What's the difference between HNSW and IVF?
faq.a: IVF partitions vectors into clusters (like bucketing), while HNSW builds a navigable graph layer by layer. HNSW typically offers better recall at the same speed; IVF is simpler to build and update. Most modern vector DBs default to HNSW.

faq.q: Does HNSW work for any vector size?
faq.a: Yes, HNSW works for any dimension. Memory overhead (graph pointers) is roughly constant per vector regardless of dimension, so high-dimensional embeddings (e.g., 1536D) don't significantly increase HNSW footprint beyond the base vector storage.

faq.q: How do I tune M and ef for HNSW?
faq.a: Start with M=16–32 and ef_construct=200 for indexing. At query time, set ef equal to your desired limit (e.g., ef=100 for top-100 neighbors). Benchmark recall@k on your data; increase both if recall is below 0.95, reduce if speed is unacceptable.

faq.q: Can I update HNSW indexes without rebuilding?
faq.a: Insertions are fast (add node, connect to M neighbors), but deletions do not compact the graph; rebuild periodically if delete rate exceeds 10–20% to reclaim memory and maintain query performance.

faq.q: Is HNSW used in all vector databases?
faq.a: HNSW dominates (Qdrant, Pinecone, pgvector, Milvus). Some use alternatives like IVF-FLAT or HNSW variants (e.g., DiskANN for disk-based search), but HNSW remains the production standard for in-memory approximate nearest-neighbor search.

title: Hybrid Search
tldr: Search combining keyword matching (BM25) and vector similarity to catch exact terms and semantic paraphrases.
plain_english: Imagine searching a manual for 'error code E-502'—a pure semantic search misses it because the code doesn't embed meaningfully. A pure keyword search misses 'car' when you ask about 'automobiles'. Hybrid search runs both methods in parallel, then fuses their rankings so you get exact matches *and* conceptual paraphrases in one result set.
how_it_works: You send a query to two parallel retrievers: one using BM25 (word frequency + document length normalization) and one using dense vectors (semantic embeddings). Each returns a ranked list. You then combine ranks using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)—a rank-agnostic method that doesn't require score normalization. The formula is: final_rank = sum(1 / (k + rank_i)) across both retrievers, with k typically set to 60. Qdrant, Weaviate, and pgvector natively support this via query APIs. The result is merged top-k results that reflect both exact-term hits and semantic closeness.
why_it_matters: For technical documentation, product support, or regulated content (where acronyms, error codes, and proper nouns matter), hybrid search recovers 10–30% more relevant results than vector-only search. You likely already have BM25 in your database—adding it alongside vector search is nearly free and fixes the most frustrating retrieval failures.
example: A support team searching an internal knowledge base for 'how to enable TLS 1.3' gets results from both a keyword match on 'TLS 1.3' *and* semantic matches on 'encryption protocol setup' or 'security configuration'. Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges these without requiring you to normalize BM25 scores (which often range 5–500) against vector cosine similarity (0–1). This avoids the brittleness of manual weighting.
pitfalls: RRF with k=60 is a legacy default—it may over-weight rare results on small corpora. Measure on your actual data and tune k downward (20–40) for better recall.
Hybrid adds noise on pure semantic queries ('explain emergence'). Use query classification (rule-based or learned) to detect term-match vs. conceptual intent and adjust fusion weights accordingly.
Combining dense *and* sparse vectors requires dual indexing overhead. If your corpus is <100k documents and lexical search alone works, hybrid adds latency without recall gains.

RRF is rank-based, not score-based, so very high-scoring outliers from one method don't dominate—but this also means fine-grained score differences are lost. Test whether reranking (a third pass) improves your use case.
when_use: Use hybrid search when your domain has domain-specific terms, product codes, or rare acronyms that must be matched exactly, or when you serve both technical and lay users asking the same conceptual query in different words.
when_avoid: Avoid hybrid when your corpus is tiny (<1000 docs), when BM25 alone already achieves >90% recall, or when you have hard latency budgets and can absorb the cost of a two-pass retrieval + rerank pipeline.

faq.q: What's the difference between hybrid search and re-ranking?
faq.a: Hybrid search fuses two parallel retrievers (keyword + vector) at the retrieval stage. Re-ranking scores an existing result set from a single retriever, using a heavier model. They often pair: retrieve with hybrid, then re-rank with a cross-encoder.

faq.q: Why use RRF instead of averaging BM25 and vector scores?
faq.a: BM25 scores (5–500+) and cosine similarity (0–1) are on different scales. RRF avoids the need to normalize them by working only with ranks, making it more stable across different datasets without manual tuning.

faq.q: Does hybrid search work with sparse vectors like SPLADE?
faq.a: Yes. You can use Reciprocal Rank Fusion with dense vectors *and* sparse vectors (e.g., SPLADE outputs) for a three-way fusion. Qdrant and Weaviate both support this natively.

faq.q: How much slower is hybrid search than vector-only search?
faq.a: Roughly 1.5–2× latency because you run two retrievals in parallel plus fusion overhead. If your BM25 is already indexed (likely), the marginal cost is one extra index lookup. Batch retrieval (Prefetch in Qdrant) keeps it sub-50ms for most setups.

faq.q: Can I tune the k parameter in RRF for my dataset?
faq.a: Yes, and you should. k=60 is a convention; lower k (20–40) weights recent ranks more heavily, raising recall. Run A/B tests on a held-out query set with your actual corpus to find the sweet spot.

title: Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE)
tldr: LLM generates a hypothetical answer to your query, which gets embedded and used to search for similar real documents.
plain_english: HyDE flips the retrieval problem: instead of embedding your short query directly, you ask an LLM to write out what a good answer would look like, then search for documents near that generated answer. It's like asking a librarian to draft a sample chapter, then finding real books that match its style and content. This works when your actual query phrasing differs wildly from how the documents are written.
how_it_works: You pass a query to an LLM with instructions to generate a plausible document or passage that answers it. That generated text gets embedded into vector space. You then run vector search against your document corpus using this synthetic embedding rather than the query's embedding. The retrieved documents are returned to the user or passed to a generation step. The trade-off: one extra LLM forward pass per query, and accuracy depends entirely on whether the LLM's generated document actually resembles your corpus.
why_it_matters: Zero-shot retrieval often fails because queries are terse ('python async tutorial') while documents are verbose ('A comprehensive guide to asynchronous programming in Python'). HyDE bridges that gap without requiring a domain-tuned embedding model or reranking, making it a cheap recall boost when your embedding model is generic.
example: A support team indexes 10,000 help articles. User searches 'how do I reset my password?' — a generic embedding model ranks poorly because most articles bury password resets in longer procedure docs. HyDE generates 'To reset your password, go to Settings, click Account Security, then select Reset Password. You'll receive an email with a confirmation link.' That synthetic doc embeds much closer to the actual help articles, lifting retrieval from rank 47 to rank 3.
pitfalls: The LLM's generated document is only as good as its training data—if it hallucinates plausible-sounding text unrelated to your domain, retrieval fails.
Adding an LLM call per query increases latency and cost; this only pays off if your embedding model is weak or your corpus phrasing is very different from query phrasing.
HyDE assumes the LLM has relevant world knowledge; for niche or proprietary domains, it may generate misleading passages that lead to wrong document clusters.
If your corpus contains many near-duplicate documents, the synthetic embedding may cluster near noisy results instead of signal.
when_use: Use HyDE when you have a weak or generic embedding model, your corpus is verbose and query phrasing differs sharply from document language, and you cannot afford to fine-tune an embedding model.
when_avoid: Avoid HyDE if latency is critical, your embedding model is already domain-tuned, your corpus is terse and matches query language naturally, or you lack confidence in the LLM's knowledge of your domain.

faq.q: Does HyDE work better than BM25?
faq.a: HyDE typically outperforms sparse retrieval (BM25) on zero-shot tasks when embedding models are strong, but it adds latency and cost. BM25 is still faster and requires no LLM; hybrid search often beats both alone.

faq.q: How much does HyDE slow down my retrieval?
faq.a: One LLM call per query. For a 100-token model running at 50 tokens/sec, expect 2–4 seconds latency added per request, depending on your LLM provider and concurrency.

faq.q: Can I use HyDE with a small or quantized LLM?
faq.a: Yes, but quality degrades. Smaller models hallucinate more and have weaker world knowledge, so the synthetic document may not resemble your corpus. Test with your actual data before deploying.

faq.q: Is HyDE the same as query expansion?
faq.a: No. Query expansion generates multiple query variants; HyDE generates a single hypothetical document. Different mechanics, but both solve the query-document mismatch problem.

faq.q: Do I need to tune HyDE or is it truly zero-shot?
faq.a: HyDE is zero-shot out of the box, but you can improve it by tuning the LLM's instructions, using a domain-aware model, or combining it with reranking for production systems.

title: IVF (Inverted File Index)
tldr: Vector index that clusters embeddings via k-means; queries search only nearby clusters instead of the full corpus.
plain_english: IVF groups your vectors into clusters before storing them, like organizing books by genre instead of dumping them in one pile. When you search, you only look inside the nearest clusters, not every single vector. This trades a bit of accuracy for much faster search times.
how_it_works: IVF runs k-means clustering on your corpus vectors to create nlist centroids (cluster centers). Each vector gets assigned to its nearest centroid. At query time, you find the nprobe nearest centroids to your query vector and search only within those clusters. The nprobe parameter controls the accuracy-speed tradeoff: higher nprobe means searching more clusters (better recall, slower), lower means fewer clusters (faster, lower recall). IVF alone is memory-heavy; combining it with product quantization (IVF-PQ) compresses vectors inside each cluster.
why_it_matters: IVF is the foundation for scaling vector search in production. FAISS, Milvus, and most vector databases use IVF variants. Understanding how to tune nprobe is critical for hitting latency budgets without tanking recall—a miscalibrated nprobe can make your RAG system either too slow or too inaccurate.
example: A customer support RAG system ingests 10 million support tickets as embeddings. Searching all 10 million vectors per query takes 2 seconds—too slow. Using IVF with 1000 clusters and nprobe=50, the system searches only ~500k vectors (5% of the corpus) and returns results in 50ms. If recall drops below threshold, the team increases nprobe to 100, trading 100ms latency for better answer quality.
pitfalls: Setting nprobe too low and missing relevant vectors; start with nprobe ≈ sqrt(nlist) and measure recall before tuning down.
Running k-means on high-dimensional data without dimensionality reduction first; PCA or product quantization should precede IVF.
Not retraining IVF centroids after corpus changes; stale clusters degrade search quality as your data distribution shifts.
Confusing IVF with HNSW; IVF is partitioning-based (deterministic cluster assignment), HNSW is graph-based (probabilistic links).
when_use: Use IVF when you have >1M vectors and need latency under 100ms; clusters are stable or you can afford periodic retraining.
when_avoid: Avoid IVF if your corpus is small (<100k vectors), your data distribution changes constantly, or you require 99.9%+ recall on every query.

faq.q: What's the difference between IVF and HNSW?
faq.a: IVF partitions vectors into static clusters via k-means; all vectors in a cluster are checked. HNSW builds a navigational graph with probabilistic links. IVF is faster for very large corpora but requires retraining; HNSW adapts incrementally but has higher memory overhead.

faq.q: How do I choose nlist and nprobe?
faq.a: Start with nlist = sqrt(corpus_size) and nprobe = sqrt(nlist). Profile your recall-latency tradeoff; increase nprobe if recall is too low, decrease if latency exceeds budget.

faq.q: Does IVF work with compressed vectors (PQ)?
faq.a: Yes—IVF-PQ combines both. IVF clusters vectors, then product quantization compresses each cluster, cutting memory by 40–90× while keeping search fast.

faq.q: What happens to IVF performance when I add new vectors?
faq.a: New vectors get assigned to existing clusters without retraining. Over time, cluster imbalance increases, degrading search quality. Periodic retraining (weekly/monthly, depending on ingestion rate) is required.

faq.q: Is IVF better than flat search for RAG?
faq.a: For <100k vectors, flat search is often faster. For >1M vectors, IVF is essential—flat search becomes a bottleneck. Choose based on corpus size and latency requirements.

title: Knowledge Graph (RAG Context)
tldr: A structured database of entities and relationships that enables multi-hop reasoning in RAG systems.
plain_english: A knowledge graph stores information as connected nodes (like people, places, concepts) and labeled edges (like "works at" or "located in"). Instead of searching only by similarity, you traverse these connections to answer questions that require linking multiple facts together. Think of it as a searchable map of how things relate to each other.
how_it_works: You extract entities and relationships from source documents using NER or an LLM, then load them into a graph database (Neo4j, TigerGraph, Neptune, or RDF stores). At query time, you can execute graph traversals (Cypher/SPARQL) to find multi-hop paths—e.g., "What companies did engineers from Project X work for?"—then combine those results with vector search for ranking. The graph handles structured reasoning; vectors handle fuzzy semantic matching.
why_it_matters: Multi-hop questions and highly connected domains (finance, biology, knowledge bases) often require explicit relationship traversal that vector-only RAG cannot reliably do. Graphs trade upfront engineering cost for precision on reasoning tasks where connections matter more than keyword similarity.
example: A pharmaceutical research team uses a knowledge graph to link drug compounds, genes, diseases, and clinical trials. When a researcher asks "Which drugs target genes associated with this disease?", the system traverses drug→gene→disease edges, then re-ranks candidates by embedding similarity. Pure vector search would miss the explicit pathway reasoning.
pitfalls: Extraction quality bottleneck: Noisy entity or relationship extraction cascades into false reasoning paths; invest in annotation or active learning.
Incomplete graph coverage: If critical relationships aren't extracted, the graph will miss valid answers while still executing faster—creating silent failures.
Schema lock-in: Changing the graph schema (adding new relationship types) requires re-processing and re-loading large corpora; design schemas conservatively.
Scalability cliff: Graph traversal can explode exponentially on dense graphs; query optimization (index strategy, depth limits) is non-trivial.
when_use: Use knowledge graphs when questions require multi-hop reasoning, domains have dense relational structure, or precision on connected reasoning outweighs latency cost.
when_avoid: Avoid knowledge graphs for simple lookup or keyword-heavy retrieval, when source data changes rapidly (high extraction cost), or when vector similarity alone solves the problem.

faq.q: How is a knowledge graph different from vector search?
faq.a: Vector search finds semantically similar text; knowledge graphs explicitly traverse relationships. Graphs excel at multi-hop reasoning (A→B→C); vectors excel at fuzzy matching. Most systems combine both.

faq.q: Can I use a knowledge graph without an LLM?
faq.a: Yes. Graph traversal and SPARQL/Cypher queries are deterministic. You can extract relationships rule-based or via NER. LLMs help with extraction quality and converting natural language to queries, but aren't required.

faq.q: What's the difference between a knowledge graph and a vector database?
faq.a: A vector database stores and searches embeddings by similarity. A knowledge graph stores explicit nodes and edges, queried by relationship paths. They solve different problems and work best together in hybrid RAG.

faq.q: How do I extract entities and relationships from raw text?
faq.a: Options: rule-based NER (spaCy, regex), supervised NER models (fine-tuned BERT), or LLM prompting. Quality varies; investment in labeling or evaluation (benchmark against a gold set) is critical.

faq.q: Does a knowledge graph work for unstructured documents?
faq.a: You must first extract structure (entities and relationships) from unstructured text. If extraction is unreliable, graph reasoning will fail silently. Hybrid RAG—combining graph + vector search—mitigates this risk.

title: Metadata Filtering (Vector DB)
tldr: Restrict vector search results to documents matching specific conditions before ranking by similarity.
plain_english: Metadata filtering narrows the pool of documents your vector database searches before computing similarity scores. Think of it like filtering a hotel search by price range before sorting by rating—you exclude non-matches first, then rank the rest. This prevents data leaks in multi-tenant systems and ensures freshness constraints are honored.
how_it_works: You attach metadata (tenant_id, created_date, category, author) to each document when indexing. At query time, you specify filter conditions (e.g., tenant_id = 'acme', created_date >= '2024-01-01'). The database applies these filters to shrink the candidate pool, then runs approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search only on matching documents, returning the top-k by similarity. Pre-filtering (filter before search) is faster and safer than post-filtering (search then filter), because post-filtering may return fewer results than requested if filtered docs rank low.
why_it_matters: Without metadata filtering, a multi-tenant RAG system leaks customer data across tenants. Filtering is your first line of defense against data isolation failures. It also enables regulatory compliance (return only documents from the current month) and performance wins (shrink the search space before expensive similarity math).
example: A financial services firm indexes 10M legal contracts with metadata: {tenant_id, doc_type, signed_date}. When Acme Corp queries their RAG, the system filters to tenant_id='acme' AND signed_date >= '2023-01-01', reducing the search pool from 10M to 50K. Then it ranks those 50K by embedding similarity. Without this filter, Acme might see Beta Corp's contracts in results.
pitfalls: Post-filtering can return fewer than k results if most high-scoring documents fail the filter, breaking pagination or client expectations.
Over-filtering with AND logic may exclude relevant documents; use OR carefully to avoid zero results.
Metadata cardinality (e.g., millions of unique tenant IDs) can degrade index performance; partition by tenant if possible.
Forgetting to filter in multi-tenant setups creates silent data leaks—audit your query patterns to confirm filters are applied.
when_use: Use metadata filtering whenever you have multi-tenant data, time-based constraints (freshness), categorical scoping (legal vs. technical docs), or access control needs.
when_avoid: Avoid metadata filtering if you have no multi-tenant or access-control requirements and your entire dataset is small enough to fit in memory; the overhead of filter parsing may outweigh benefits.

faq.q: What's the difference between metadata filtering and post-filtering?
faq.a: Metadata filtering applies constraints before the vector search runs, shrinking the candidate pool. Post-filtering applies constraints after, which can return fewer results than requested. Pre-filtering is faster and more predictable.

faq.q: Do all vector databases support metadata filtering?
faq.a: Most production vector DBs (Qdrant, Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus) support it natively. Simple or in-memory stores may not. Check the database documentation for filter syntax and performance.

faq.q: Can I filter by multiple conditions at once?
faq.a: Yes. Most vector DBs support AND, OR, and NOT operators. Use AND to narrow scope (safe for multi-tenancy) and OR to broaden (risky if it crosses tenant boundaries).

faq.q: How does metadata filtering affect query latency?
faq.a: Well-indexed metadata filters reduce latency by shrinking the search space. Poorly indexed or high-cardinality metadata (e.g., unique user IDs) can slow queries. Test with your data volume.

faq.q: Is metadata filtering enough for data isolation in a multi-tenant RAG?
faq.a: It's necessary but not sufficient. Combine it with access control checks in your application layer, encryption at rest, and audit logging to ensure true isolation.

acceptedAnswer.text: Well-indexed metadata filters reduce latency by shrinking the search space. Poorly indexed or high-cardinality metadata (e.g., unique user IDs) can slow queries. Test with your data volume.

mainEntity.name: Is metadata filtering enough for data isolation in a multi-tenant RAG?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's necessary but not sufficient. Combine it with access control checks in your application layer, encryption at rest, and audit logging to ensure true isolation.

title: Multi-Query Retrieval
tldr: LLM rewrites a query multiple ways, retrieves for each variant, and merges results to improve recall.
plain_english: Instead of searching once, you ask the LLM to generate 3–5 different phrasings of the same question. Each phrasing gets retrieved independently, then all results are combined and deduplicated. It's like asking a librarian to search under 'dog', 'canine', 'puppy', and 'hound'—you'll find documents the first search alone would miss.
how_it_works: You pass the user's query to an LLM with a prompt like: 'Generate 5 alternative ways to search for this question.' The LLM outputs variations. Each variant runs through your retriever (BM25, dense embedding, or hybrid). Results are merged using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) or score averaging, which weights documents higher if they appear in multiple result sets. Duplicates are removed. The final merged list is passed to the LLM for generation.
why_it_matters: Query ambiguity and phrasing mismatches are common recall killers in RAG. Multi-query fixes both without retraining your retriever. It's cheap engineering relative to the recall gain—most teams see 5–15% improvement in hit rate. Trade-off: you pay for N retrievals instead of one, which increases latency and token cost proportionally.
example: A customer-support RAG system receives 'How do I reset my password?' Multi-query generates: 'forgotten password recovery', 'change account credentials', 'sign-in issues', 'account access'. Each retrieves from your docs. One variant matches an old help article titled 'Credential Recovery Guide'. The merged result set ranks it high because it appears in multiple retrieval results, even though the user's original query didn't use those exact terms.
pitfalls: LLM-generated queries can be off-topic or redundant if the prompt is loose; bad queries in the ensemble degrade rather than improve recall.
Reciprocal rank fusion requires tuning the fusion method and number of variants; no single setting works for all domains.
Cost multiplies with number of variants and retriever latency; 5 variants on a slow retriever can make RAG unusable.
Duplicate handling is critical; naive merging can rank the same document multiple times, wasting slots.
when_use: Use multi-query when your recall is below 70%, users phrase questions in multiple ways, or your domain has synonyms and technical jargon.
when_avoid: Avoid multi-query if latency is critical (<200ms), your retriever already achieves >85% recall, or queries are simple and unambiguous.

faq.q: How many query variants should I generate?
faq.a: 3–5 is typical. Fewer misses gains; more than 5 rarely improves recall meaningfully and burns latency and tokens. Tune on your eval set.

faq.q: Is multi-query the same as query rewriting?
faq.a: No. Query rewriting fixes a single query; multi-query generates many. Query rewriting is preprocessing; multi-query is ensemble retrieval.

faq.q: Can I use multi-query with vector databases?
faq.a: Yes. Multi-query works with any retriever—BM25, dense embeddings, hybrid, or graph-based. The variants just need to work with your backend.

faq.q: What if the LLM generates gibberish queries?
faq.a: Add examples and constraints to your prompt. Use JSON schema enforcement or guided generation to ensure variants are valid. Test on a validation set first.

faq.q: Does multi-query replace a reranker?
faq.a: No. Multi-query improves recall by retrieving more relevant docs; a reranker improves precision by re-ranking them. Use both for best results.
